text.title: Cost Estimation Framework
text.summary: Five-step process for building a defensible cost baseline: categorize costs, estimate bottom-up, apply PERT uncertainty, add risk-driven contingency, and separate management reserve.

section.title: Cost Categories

rule.statement: Classify every cost line into one of four categories before summing: direct, indirect, fixed, variable.
rule.rationale: Mixing categories causes budget gaps; indirect costs (overhead, management) are the most-often forgotten and typically add 30-50% above direct labor.

section.p: Direct: salaries, contractors, software licenses. Indirect: office overhead, utilities, management loading. Fixed: base salaries, subscriptions. Variable: overtime, cloud usage, contractor hours.

rule.statement: Estimate each WBS work package independently: identify roles, apply hours, multiply by fully-loaded rate (salary x 1.3-1.8 depending on tax jurisdiction).
rule.rationale: Bottom-up is more accurate than analogical or parametric when scope is known; it forces explicit decomposition that surfaces missing work packages.

rule.statement: Always cite the source of every rate (vendor quote, internal rate card version, market survey). Reject any estimate line that lacks a rate source.
rule.rationale: LLMs and humans both invent plausible rates; without a source the number cannot be defended or audited.

rule.statement: For uncertain cost lines, collect three estimates (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) and compute Expected = (O + 4M + P) / 6.
rule.rationale: Single-point estimates anchor on optimistic outcomes; PERT forces acknowledgment of the worst case and weights the most-likely scenario correctly.

rule.statement: If Pessimistic / Most Likely is less than 1.8, flag the estimate for human review — pessimistic values are systematically underestimated.
rule.rationale: Anchoring bias causes humans and LLMs to set pessimistic estimates too close to most-likely; a ratio below 1.8 is a signal the tail risk was not considered.

example.note: Cloud hosting 3-point: O=$200/mo, M=$350/mo, P=$800/mo. Expected = (200 + 4x350 + 800) / 6 = $400/mo. Ratio P/M = 2.3, passes the review gate.

section.title: Step 3: Contingency Reserve (Known Unknowns)

rule.statement: Size contingency reserve using the risk register: sum each open risk's (probability x impact), then round up. Do not use a flat percentage.
rule.rationale: Flat percentages (10-15%) ignore actual risk distribution; risk-driven reserves are defensible to sponsors and scale correctly with project uncertainty.

section.p: Guidance when no risk register exists: 10-15% for well-defined scope; 20-25% for innovative projects; 30-40% for high-uncertainty work. These are starting points, not substitutes for a register.

rule.statement: Contingency reserve is PM-controlled; document it in the Cost Baseline. Management reserve is sponsor-controlled and stays outside the baseline.
rule.rationale: Blending the two removes the governance layer that protects the project from scope escalation without authorization.

rule.statement: Cost Baseline = Direct Costs + Indirect Costs + Contingency Reserve. BAC = Cost Baseline + Management Reserve.
rule.rationale: Standard PMI formula; separating the two allows EVM tracking (CPI, SPI) against the baseline while keeping major-scope buffer separate.

rule.statement: Re-baseline at every approved change-control event; version the baseline with the change-request ID.
rule.rationale: Never letting the original estimate persist past a scope change is the only way to maintain a valid audit trail.

text.reference: PERT mean, std-dev, P80, P95 from WBS JSON
